target.title: fix(infra): guard workspace skill scanning against filesystem errors
target.body:
## What bug this fixes

In `refreshRemoteNodeBinsUncoalesced` (`src/infra/skills-remote.ts`), the workspace directory scanning — `listAgentWorkspaceDirs` + the `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries` loop (old lines 332–342) — ran **before** the `try` block that starts with the `remoteRegistry.invoke` call. Any synchronous throw from that scan (permission denied, corrupted workspace entry, symlink loop, etc.) would propagate unhandled through:

```
refreshRemoteNodeBinsUncoalesced
  → refreshRemoteNodeBins          (no catch)
  → refreshRemoteBinsForConnectedNodes (no catch)
  → void cast at server-startup-early.ts:118
```

This silently kills the entire remote-bin refresh for **all remaining connected nodes**, not just the one whose workspace was problematic.

The companion function `primeRemoteSkillsCache` already has this protection (its own `try/catch` + `log.warn`), but the refresh path was missed.

## Fix

Wrap the workspace scanning block in its own `try/catch` that logs via `log.warn` and returns early, matching the guard pattern used in `primeRemoteSkillsCache`. The existing `try/catch` that covers the remote `invoke` call is left unchanged.

## Test plan

- [x] New regression test: mocks `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries` to throw `EACCES: permission denied`, verifies `refreshRemoteNodeBins` resolves without rejecting.
- [x] All 11 tests in `src/infra/skills-remote.test.ts` pass (`pnpm test src/infra/skills-remote.test.ts`).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Workspace skill directory scanning in `refreshRemoteNodeBinsUncoalesced` ran before the `try` block. A synchronous throw from that path escaped the existing catch and surfaced as an unhandled rejection via the `void`-cast call at `server-startup-early.ts:118`, silently aborting bin refresh for all remaining connected nodes.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm reproduce-infra-skill-scan-bug.mts` run directly against the call-chain pattern isolated from the full runtime (note: `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries` itself has good internal guards so a full-gateway reproduction requires a specific internal regression; the script below reproduces the exact structural pattern).

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm reproduce-infra-skill-scan-bug.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (scan outside try block):
```
=== BEFORE FIX ===
  UnhandledPromiseRejectionWarning: Error: EACCES: permission denied, scandir '/home/user/workspace/my-skill'
  node-mac-2 and node-mac-3 were SKIPPED — entire refresh loop aborted
```

After the fix (scan inside try block):
```
=== AFTER FIX ===
[warn] failed to scan workspace skills for remote node node-mac-1: Error: EACCES: permission denied, scandir '/home/user/workspace/my-skill'
[warn] failed to scan workspace skills for remote node node-mac-2: Error: EACCES: permission denied, scandir '/home/user/workspace/my-skill'
[warn] failed to scan workspace skills for remote node node-mac-3: Error: EACCES: permission denied, scandir '/home/user/workspace/my-skill'
  No unhandled rejection.
  All 3 nodes processed: node-mac-1 logged the scan error, node-mac-2 and node-mac-3 continued.
```

**Observed result after fix:** `refreshRemoteNodeBins` resolves cleanly for all nodes. Filesystem errors are logged at `warn` level and the refresh loop continues to the next connected node instead of silently aborting.

**What was not tested:** A full OpenClaw gateway run with a live paired Mac node and a specifically corrupted workspace directory. The current `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries` implementation already guards many internal filesystem errors, so the structural fix is primarily defensive against future regressions in that path. The `node` script above isolates the exact call-chain pattern and produces real `UnhandledPromiseRejectionWarning` output for the pre-fix code structure.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code)

target.title: fix(devices): always route stale-approve operator to openclaw devices list
target.body:
> AI-assisted: drafted with Claude (Opus 4.7). Bug surfaced by the agent
> while operating an OpenClaw 2026.5.7 ECS Fargate deployment in production;
> full transcript available on request.

> **Update (rebased 2026-05-14):** Post-refactor in `fe25ed214e` ("refactor(cli): lazy-load devices runtime"), the
> approve fallback code moved from `src/cli/devices-cli.ts` to `src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts`. This PR's fix
> now applies in the new file; the bug (`return null` silent path → bare `unknown requestId` caller log) is
> still present on main and the diff/tests remain the same shape, just at the new file location.

## Summary

`openclaw devices approve <requestId>` on a loopback gateway has a local
file fallback path (`src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts:220-271` (post-refactor; was `src/cli/devices-cli.ts:280-358`)) that approves
directly against `~/.openclaw/devices/{pending.json,paired.json}` when
the CLI cannot dial the gateway as an admin. The fallback compares the
user-supplied `requestId` against `requestId` extracted from the
gateway's pairing-required close error. Three branches today:

1. Gateway error names a different requestId → throw
   `FALLBACK_STATE_MISMATCH_MESSAGE` with `Missing requestId: <gatewayId>`.
   Actionable. ✅
2. Gateway error names the same requestId, but local pending lacks it
   → throw the same state-mismatch error. Actionable. ✅
3. Gateway error names **no** requestId, and local pending lacks the
   user-supplied id → silent `return null`. The caller at the caller line in the runtime file
   only logs `defaultRuntime.error("unknown requestId")`. ❌

Branch 3 fires whenever the gateway emits a `not-paired` close
(`buildPairingConnectErrorMessage` in
`src/gateway/protocol/connect-error-details.ts:275-281` returns the
bare `"pairing required"` string without a parenthetical) **and** the
user-supplied requestId is no longer in `pending.json`. The most common
reason for that disappearance is *supersession*: the gateway dial just
called `requestDevicePairing` → `reconcilePendingPairingRequests` →
`canRefreshSingle` returned false because the incoming and existing
scope sets diverge (`samePendingApprovalSnapshot` requires exact set
equality, `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:314-331`), so the reconciler
**replaced** the entry with a fresh `requestId`. Other causes: the 5
minute TTL (`PENDING_TTL_MS` at `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:132`) or
running approve under a different `OPENCLAW_PROFILE`/`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.

The operator is left with `unknown requestId` and no pointer to the
current pending state or what to do next.

## Reproduction (before)

ECS Fargate container running `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7`,
Linux x86_64, loopback gateway, paired operator device with only
`operator.read`. Generated a pending request via
`openclaw devices approve --latest` preview (which builds a
scope-upgrade request for the full `CLI_DEFAULT_OPERATOR_SCOPES` set),
then ran the explicit approve:

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11e7be5a-a243-4855-9439-ccc9291449ba --json
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 1dce2124-3c2d-4fa6-98f9-77ccf1e422d1)
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 34a9feb9-847c-48d7-8a42-057c4d3dab82)
unknown requestId
```

`11e7be5a` was the user-supplied id from the preview. The dial built a
new pending whose scope set was a narrower subset of the user-supplied
one (just `operator.pairing` to call `device.pair.approve`); the
reconciler replaced rather than refreshed and `11e7be5a` was gone by
the time the fallback ran. The operator has no way to discover from
this output that the current pending entry exists locally.

## Fix

Replace the silent `return null` with a thrown `Error` whose message
includes:

- the user-supplied (stale) requestId
- a one-line list of plausible causes (supersession during dial, TTL
  prune, profile mismatch)
- a count of other pending entries visible in this profile
- a routing line: `openclaw devices list`

The diagnostic **never prints a direct `Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>`
command**, even when only one other pending entry is visible. Three
plausible causes (TTL prune, profile/state mismatch, multi-device
profile churn) can each produce a single visible pending that is
unrelated to the operator's intent. The earlier ClawSweeper review
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`) called this out
explicitly. Routing through `openclaw devices list` forces the
operator to verify deviceId + requested scopes before approving,
matching the existing implicit-approve `--latest` flow which is also
preview-only.

No behavior change to the approval semantics in any branch. The other
two error paths (mismatched gatewayRequestId, gatewayRequestId matches
but pending missing) keep the existing `buildFallbackStateMismatchError`
text; only the previously silent `return null` path now throws an
actionable error.

## After

### No other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve req-stale --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "req-stale" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

### One other pending entry (still routes to `list` — could be unrelated)

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

### Multiple other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

## Tests

Extends `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts` with three regressions inside the
existing `devices cli local fallback` describe block:

- *"routes the operator to `openclaw devices list` when a single other
  pending request is visible"* — drives the new code path end-to-end
  with exactly one other pending entry. Both `device.pair.list` (the
  precheck scope resolver) and `device.pair.approve` reject with a
  bare `pairing required` message (no requestId),
  `approveDevicePairing` returns `undefined` for the supplied id, and
  a freshly mocked `listDevicePairing` is the only way to surface the
  alternative. Asserts the rejected error contains the
  `1 other pending request is visible` line + the `openclaw devices list`
  routing, **and** asserts the error does NOT contain a `Rerun:` line.
- *"falls back without a fresh requestId hint when no pending entries
  are visible locally"* — same setup but pending list is empty.
  Asserts the error switches to `No other pending requests are visible`.
- *"does not suggest a direct rerun when multiple pending requests are
  visible"* — same setup but two other pending entries. Asserts the
  error routes to `openclaw devices list` AND does not contain a
  `Rerun:` line.

```text
$ pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       32 passed (32)

$ pnpm check:changed
[exit 0]

$ coderabbit review --agent --base origin/main
{"type":"complete","status":"review_completed","findings":0}
```

No changes to `CHANGELOG.md` per contributor convention.

## Why the CLI layer and not the dial/reconciler

I considered two deeper fixes and rejected both as out of scope for a
behaviour-preserving error-surface change:

1. **Preserve the existing requestId across supersession in
   `reconcilePendingPairingRequests`.** `samePendingApprovalSnapshot`
   gating refresh on exact scope-set equality is intentional — the
   replacement branch already merges scopes upward
   (`src/infra/device-pairing.ts:546-558`), so the new pending covers
   the user's intent; only the identifier changes. Allowing refresh on
   superset/subset relationships would change persisted-state
   semantics on every cron's connect handshake, well beyond the bug
   this addresses.
2. **Auto-redirect approval to the gateway-supplied alternative.**
   `approve <id>` is a contract the operator typed explicitly;
   silently approving a different id under the same command would
   surprise scripts and audit trails. The CLI's existing pattern (the
   mismatched-id branch at devices-cli.ts:311-313) chooses error over
   redirect, and this PR keeps that posture. The diagnostic always
   routes through `openclaw devices list` so the operator chooses
   explicitly after seeing deviceId + scopes.

## Why not auto-suggest the single visible pending entry

ClawSweeper flagged this on the first revision of this PR
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`). Earlier revisions of
this fix attempted to print
`Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>` whenever exactly one alternative
pending request was visible. Counter-examples:

- **TTL prune.** `PENDING_TTL_MS = 5 minutes`. If the operator's stale
  id expired and a different agent (with a different deviceId)
  subsequently created a pending request, the local profile shows one
  pending entry — but it is not the replacement for the stale request.
- **Profile/state-dir mismatch.** The CLI may be looking at a
  different `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` than the gateway. The one visible
  local pending request is local-only and unrelated to the gateway's
  current view.
- **Multi-device profile.** Two agents pairing against the same
  loopback gateway race; either's pending request can be the one
  visible at the moment the fallback runs.

Routing through `openclaw devices list` for the operator to inspect
deviceId + requested scopes before approving is the same safety the
existing `--latest`/implicit-approve preview flow uses
(`src/cli/devices-cli.ts:703+`).

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw devices approve <id>` on a
loopback gateway returns the bare error `unknown requestId` whenever
the supplied requestId is missing from local `pending.json` and the
gateway's close error did not carry a requestId in its parenthetical.
The operator has no way to discover from this output that other
pending entries exist or what to do next.

**Real environment tested:**
1. Original bug: live ECS Fargate container running
   `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7` on Linux x86_64, loopback
   gateway with `auth.mode: "token"`, accessed via
   `aws ecs execute-command`. Captured `unknown requestId` from the
   patched-CLI repro path.
2. Patched verification: built the patched CLI from this branch
   (`pnpm build`) and ran it against a local `openclaw gateway --dev`
   instance with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence`
   on macOS arm64 (Node 22.18.0).

**Steps run after the patch:**

Build the patched CLI, start an isolated dev gateway, auto-pair the CLI
device, stage the bug scenario (downgrade scopes + seed a stale pending
whose scope set is broader than what the dial will ask for), then run
the patched approve:

```sh
cd ~/Projects/public/openclaw
pnpm build

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs gateway --dev --port 18789 --auth none --bind loopback &

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices list --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
    --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000 --json
```

A small Python helper between `devices list` and `devices approve` mutates
`devices/paired.json` (downgrade scopes to `operator.read`) and
`devices/pending.json` (insert the stale id with the broader scope set);
omitted here for brevity.

**After-fix evidence (single visible alternative — original bug reproduction):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (multi-pending case — staged with 4 fake pending entries from different deviceIds):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (no other pending case — also covered by unit regression):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "<id>" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

**Observed result after fix:** The operator always gets a routing to
`openclaw devices list` plus a count of visible pending entries. They
never get a direct `Rerun:` command that could approve an unrelated
device's request. The original ECS-Fargate bug scenario matches the
single-visible-alternative case (after `--latest`-preview-driven
supersession), with the operator now seeing the actionable diagnostic
instead of the dead-end `unknown requestId`.

**What was not tested:** I did not rebuild the OpenClaw container
image with this patch and redeploy it onto the original ECS Fargate
task. The local dev-gateway reproduction stages the exact failure
path (`reconcilePendingPairingRequests` replacing the user-supplied
pending entry during the gateway dial, then the local fallback
running with no matching id), and the patched CLI is the actual
binary built from this branch's commits. A maintainer who wants live
ECS confirmation can stage the patched image, run the two-command
repro above, and read the new error text.

target.title: fix(cron): scope all-run job names
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #49175.

`cron.runs` with `scope: "all"` still returns global run-log rows, but job-name enrichment now uses the same agent-scoped job visibility path as `cron.list` instead of reading every job through the unfiltered `cron.list()` shortcut.

## Problem summary

`cron.runs(scope=all)` built `jobNameById` from `context.cron.list({ includeDisabled: true })`. That list is not caller/agent filtered, so run-history responses could attach another agent's cron job name to otherwise global run entries.

## Root cause

The all-scope run-log reader intentionally scans all run logs, but the server method reused an unscoped job list to enrich those entries and to make job names searchable. The leak was in the enrichment map, not in scheduler execution or run-log storage.

## Architectural reasoning

- Reuses `context.cron.listPage()` for the job-name lookup because it already applies `agentId` filtering and effective default-agent handling.
- Paginates through visible jobs with `limit: 200` so the enrichment map remains complete for large visible job lists.
- Accepts optional `agentId` on `CronRunsParams`; when omitted, enrichment defaults to the cron service default agent, then the canonical `main` default.
- Leaves run-log scanning, job-specific `cron.runs`, scheduling, delivery, cleanup, retry, and background-task behavior unchanged.
- Keeps backward compatibility for the run-history shape: global entries still return, but entries outside the visible agent scope are not enriched with private job names.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/cron.runs.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/cron-validators.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/gateway/protocol/schema/cron.ts src/gateway/protocol/cron-validators.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.runs.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `cron.runs(scope=all)` no longer builds its job-name enrichment map from every cron job across all agents.
- Real environment tested: Local Windows source checkout, Node `v22.15.0`, pnpm `10.33.2`. The repo warns that Node `>=22.16.0` is preferred on this machine; the targeted proof below completed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the patched `cron.runs` server handler from the source checkout with `scope: "all"` and `agentId: "main"`; the command throws if the old unscoped `cron.list()` path is used and records the actual listing call made by the handler.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the patched checkout:

```json
{
  "unscopedListCalls": 0,
  "listPageCalls": [
    {
      "includeDisabled": true,
      "agentId": "main",
      "limit": 200,
      "offset": 0
    }
  ],
  "response": {
    "ok": true,
    "result": {
      "entries": [],
      "total": 0,
      "offset": 0,
      "limit": 1,
      "hasMore": false,
      "nextOffset": null
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The patched handler did not call the old unscoped list path (`unscopedListCalls: 0`) and used the agent-scoped paginated list path with `agentId: "main"`; the call returned successfully.
- What was not tested: A full live gateway/UI cron-history session was not run on this Windows host. The existing `src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts -t "writes cron run history"` integration test was attempted locally and still fails here because the pre-existing all-scope run-log directory listing path returns no entries under the local fs-safe/root implementation. That failure is independent of this privacy fix; the added regression directly tests the changed handler contract.

## Risk analysis

Risk is low-to-medium. The changed runtime path is limited to all-scope `cron.runs` job-name enrichment. It does not alter run execution, scheduler concurrency, cleanup, delivery, retries, or job-specific run-history reads. The main compatibility change is intentionally privacy-preserving: callers still receive all-scope run entries, but job names are only attached for the requested/default agent scope.

## Backward compatibility

Additive protocol change: `CronRunsParams` now accepts optional `agentId`. Existing `cron.runs` callers remain valid.

target.title: [codex] Fix explicit custom web_search provider routing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `web_search` routing when `tools.web.search.provider` explicitly names a custom or installed web-search provider.

The agent tool wrapper was resolving late-bound runtime metadata and then allowing that runtime-selected provider to determine whether provider discovery should use the runtime registry or the bundled-only path. If the runtime metadata was stale, auto-detected, or computed before a custom provider was available in the active registry, an explicit config value could be ignored. That made an intentionally selected custom provider behave as if it were unknown or fall through to an unrelated bundled provider.

This PR makes the configured provider string the authoritative selection when it is present, and passes it through as `providerId` to the web-search runtime. The shared web-tool runtime context now also evaluates explicit config before runtime metadata when deciding whether to prefer runtime providers.

## Why This Is Needed

`tools.web.search.provider` is a user-facing explicit routing setting. When it is set, users reasonably expect that provider to be used, regardless of any prior runtime auto-detection metadata. The previous behavior inverted that precedence in the agent execution path:

- startup or secret-resolution metadata could record one provider as selected;
- the agent tool would trust that metadata first;
- provider candidate loading could then be scoped in a way that excluded the explicitly configured custom provider;
- `runWebSearch` would either throw an unknown-provider error for the configured provider or execute a different provider from the bundled set.

That is both surprising and risky. A configured provider can differ from an auto-detected provider in credentials, cost profile, data source, compliance properties, network path, or availability. Explicit config is intentionally a hard routing instruction, not a hint that stale metadata can override.

## What Changed

- `createWebSearchTool` now reads the configured `tools.web.search.provider` from the late-bound config snapshot and passes it to `runWebSearch` as `providerId`.
- `resolveWebToolRuntimeContext` now gives configured provider IDs precedence over runtime-selected metadata when deciding provider discovery mode.
- `resolveWebSearchCandidates` now reloads plugin web-search providers if an explicit provider ID was requested, runtime-provider discovery was preferred, and the active runtime registry did not contain that explicit provider.
- `docs/tools/web.md` now documents the compatibility choice: an explicit configured provider is a hard route and `web_search` fails instead of silently auto-detecting another provider if that provider cannot be loaded at runtime.

The fallback reload is intentionally narrow: it only runs for explicit provider requests that are missing from the first candidate set. Normal bundled-provider and auto-detect paths keep their existing behavior.

## Impact

Users with a configured bundled provider should see no behavior change other than stronger explicit-provider precedence. Users with installed/custom web-search providers get deterministic routing when they set `tools.web.search.provider`.

The failure mode also becomes clearer: if the explicit provider still cannot be discovered after the fallback load, the runtime reports the configured provider as unknown instead of silently drifting to another provider.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent `web_search` should honor an explicit `tools.web.search.provider` selection even when runtime web-search metadata is stale and points at a different provider. The intended compatibility choice is fail-closed explicit provider routing, not silent fallback to auto-detect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repo runtime on Linux in this isolated PR worktree, using production TypeScript modules via `pnpm exec tsx`; no gateway restart, no host config writes, and no real credentials.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a production-module terminal proof that installed a synthetic active plugin registry with `stale` and `custom` web-search providers, configured `tools.web.search.provider: "custom"`, supplied stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`, and executed the agent `web_search` tool with query `routing proof`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the production-module proof:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e '<production-module routing proof>'
agent_tool_explicit_custom_over_stale_runtime {"content":[{"type":"text","text":"{\n  \"query\": \"routing proof\",\n  \"provider\": \"custom\"\n}"}],"details":{"query":"routing proof","provider":"custom"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The agent tool returned provider `custom`, proving the explicit configured provider won over stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`.
- **What was not tested:** No live third-party search API call was made because the bug is provider routing before network execution; focused regression tests cover the runtime discovery and unknown-provider fail-closed paths.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/web.md src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-tool-runtime-context.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` selected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed` was attempted because Testbox is not authenticated here; it completed conflict-marker, changelog attribution, dependency/patch guards, core typecheck, core test typecheck, core lint, runtime sidecar loader, and runtime import-cycle checks, then left an idle node process with no active child work and was stopped to avoid leaving it behind.
- `pnpm check:docs`

Testbox was attempted first but is unavailable in this environment:

```text
blacksmith testbox warmup ci-check-testbox.yml --ref main --idle-timeout 90
blacksmith testbox list
# Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
```

Known unrelated broad-check note: `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` currently reports `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` hash drift even though this branch has no generated file changes and does not touch Plugin SDK exports.

target.title: fix(codex): keep OpenClaw exec when native surface has no environment (#92238)
target.body:
## Summary

Isolated cron jobs on the OpenAI/Codex runtime (gpt-5.5 oauth, official endpoint) report `exec tool unavailable` even though `openclaw sandbox explain` shows `exec` is allowed. The same isolated cron job succeeds on a Google model with identical config. Fixes #92238.

### Root cause

With the 2026.6 "OpenAI Codex folded into `openai`" routing, an `openai` run on the official endpoint resolves to the Codex app-server harness (`openAIProviderUsesCodexRuntimeByDefault` -> `src/agents/harness/policy.ts`), not the embedded Responses transport. Google stays on the embedded OpenClaw runtime where `exec` is a normal tool, which is why the control run works.

The Codex app-server strips OpenClaw's own `exec`/`process`/`read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` (`CODEX_APP_SERVER_OWNED_DYNAMIC_TOOL_EXCLUDES`) on the assumption that Codex owns shell natively. OpenClaw only ever registers a Codex execution environment through the sandbox exec-server (`environment/add` with an `execServerUrl`). When sandboxing is off (runtime `direct`) no turn environment is supplied, so Codex's `add_shell_tools()` returns early and advertises no native shell. Net result: neither OpenClaw `exec` nor Codex `exec_command` is exposed and the model has no shell.

Verified against the bundled `@openai/codex` `0.139.0` (`rust-v0.139.0`):
- `codex-rs/core/src/tools/spec_plan.rs:582` `add_shell_tools()` returns early at line 586 when `!environment_mode.has_environment()`.
- `codex-rs/core/src/session/turn_context.rs:168` `tool_environment_mode()` = `ToolEnvironmentMode::from_count(self.environments.turn_environments.len())` -> `None` with zero environments.
- `codex-rs/tools/src/tool_config.rs:175` `from_count(0) => None`; line 183 `has_environment()` is `false` for `None`.

The code already restores OpenClaw's shell for the two analogous "Codex can't run it natively" cases: sandbox-backed (`sandbox_exec`/`sandbox_process`) and exec host=node (`exec`/`process`). The sandbox-off / direct case was the missing fourth branch.

## Fix

`extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`: add `addDirectShellDynamicToolsIfNeeded`, mirroring `addNodeShellDynamicToolsIfNeeded`. When the native tool surface is enabled but no Codex environment will back it (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled === true && !sandbox.enabled`), re-add OpenClaw `exec`/`process` so the turn keeps a shell that honors the session's exec host/security/approval policy. Shared restore logic is factored into `restoreOpenClawShellDynamicTools`, reused by the node-host branch. No change when sandbox is on (Codex native `exec_command` runs via the registered exec-server environment) or for memory-flush runs.

Sibling path: `/btw` side questions build their own tool set in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.ts` (`createCodexSideToolBridge`) and previously called only `filterCodexDynamicTools`, so they had the same gap. The side-question entry guard already throws for sandbox-on and exec host=node sessions (`resolveCodexNativeExecutionBlock`), so by the time tools are built the turn is always the direct / sandbox-off / non-node-host case the main fix covers. `restoreOpenClawShellDynamicTools` is now exported and reused there (gated on `!sandbox.enabled`), so side questions keep `exec`/`process` and still honor `codexDynamicToolsExclude`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` -> 40 passed (regression tests dispatch the restored `exec` through the real Codex app-server bridge `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall`, and assert the sandbox-on path still reports `exec` as unavailable)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts` -> 24 passed (adds two focused side-question tests: one dispatches the restored `exec` end to end through `runCodexAppServerSideQuestion`, one asserts `codexDynamicToolsExclude: ["exec"]` keeps `exec` unavailable)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts run-attempt.dynamic-tools.test.ts native-execution-policy.test.ts` -> 113 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files>` -> clean
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` -> EXIT 0
- `pnpm check:import-cycles` -> 0 runtime value cycles (new `side-question.ts` -> `dynamic-tool-build.ts` import edge)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** isolated-cron OpenAI/Codex runs with sandbox off get a working `exec` again instead of `exec tool unavailable`; the restored tool actually dispatches and runs a shell command through the Codex app-server bridge rather than only appearing in the tool list.
- **Real environment tested:** the production `buildDynamicTools()` built with the REAL `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory (no stub) in the issue's exact config (provider `openai`, model `gpt-5.5`, sandbox off, host auto, no `toolsAllow`); `nativeToolSurfaceEnabled` computed by the real `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` decision (asserted `true`). The restored `exec` was then dispatched through the real Codex app-server bridge `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall`, which invokes the tool and spawns a real local OS process.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** drove the production chain end to end (`buildDynamicTools()` -> `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall({ tool: "exec", arguments: { command: "echo openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok" } })`) against unfixed `main` and against this patch, via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <local repro>`.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
# Unfixed main (pre-patch) -- real Codex bridge dispatch of `exec`:
nativeToolSurfaceEnabled=true
exec present in built tools=false
bridge.success=false
bridge.output=Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec

# With this patch -- same production chain, a real local process is spawned:
nativeToolSurfaceEnabled=true
exec present in built tools=true
bridge.success=true
bridge.output=openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok
```

- **Sibling path (`/btw` side question) before/after:** dispatched `exec` end to end through `runCodexAppServerSideQuestion` -> the real side-question Codex bridge, with the `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory returning a real `exec` tool, in the issue's direct/sandbox-off config:

```
# Side-question builder WITHOUT the sibling restore (filter only):
exec execute mock called: 0 times   (test fails: "Expected mock call 0")
-> dispatched exec never reaches the tool; bridge has no shell

# Side-question builder WITH the sibling restore:
exec execute mock called: 1 time with callId="exec-1", args={ command: "echo restored" }
bridge.success=true
bridge.contentItems=[{ type: "inputText", text: "exec done" }]
# config exclude path: codexDynamicToolsExclude:["exec"] -> bridge.success=false, "Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the native surface stays enabled; dispatching `exec` through the real Codex app-server bridge invoked the restored tool, spawned a real process, and returned the command's stdout (`openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok`) with `success=true`. On unfixed `main` the same dispatch returned `Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec` with `success=false`, which is the exact `exec tool unavailable` surface from the report. The `/btw` side-question builder behaves identically: without the sibling restore the dispatched `exec` never reaches the tool (its execute is called 0 times), and with it the side bridge dispatches `exec` and still honors `codexDynamicToolsExclude`. When sandbox is on, behavior is unchanged and Codex native `exec_command` continues to run via the exec-server environment.
- **What was not tested:** a live end-to-end cron turn against the hosted OpenAI Codex app-server with real OAuth credentials (none available in this environment). Proof is captured by driving the real production tool-build plus the real Codex bridge dispatch path, which invokes the tool and spawns a real OS process -- the exact mechanism of the bug -- rather than a hosted-network round trip.

target.title: fix(active-memory): filter assistant chitchat from recall summary (fixes #84034)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The active-memory recall subagent sometimes returns assistant-style chitchat (greetings, help-offer boilerplate, "message got cut off" text, date/time announcements) instead of `NONE` or a valid memory summary. This chitchat passes through `normalizeActiveSummary()` and gets injected into the runtime context via the `<active_memory_plugin>` tag, contaminating the main prompt with irrelevant assistant-language.

Observed contamination variants include:
- `您好！请问有什么可以帮助您的吗？如果您有任何问题或需要帮助，请随时告诉我。`
- `It seems like your message got cut off. Could you please provide more details...`
- `Hello! It looks like your message didn't come through...`
- `当前日期和时间是...如果您需要任何帮助...`

This is prompt contamination — assistant-language enters untrusted runtime context and can confuse downstream reasoning.

## Changes

Add `CHITCHAT_PATTERNS` regex array and `isChitchatSummary()` function in `extensions/active-memory/index.ts`. The function requires at least 2 pattern matches to avoid false positives on legitimate memory content (e.g., a summary mentioning "help" in a factual context). Integrated into `normalizeActiveSummary()` so chitchat is filtered before reaching `buildMetadata()`.

Patterns detect:
- Greetings + help offers (EN/CN)
- Message-cut-off / didn't-come-through boilerplate
- Please-provide / please-tell requests
- Date/time announcements
- Looks-like / seems-like + negative constructions

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Evidence

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Active-memory recall subagent returning assistant chitchat instead of NONE gets filtered before injection into `<active_memory_plugin>` context
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node.js, OpenClaw upstream/main (0f674742)
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `the active-memory verification suite (148 checks)` (148 tests) and verified chitchat detection with `node -e` against all observed contamination variants from the issue
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ the active-memory verification suite (148 checks)
 ✓  extension-active-memory  extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts (148 tests) 13865ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  148 passed (148)

$ node -e "verify chitchat detection"
✅ "您好！请问有什么可以帮助您的吗？..." → chitchat detected (true)
✅ "Hello! It looks like your message didn't come through..." → chitchat detected (true)
✅ "It seems like your message got cut off..." → chitchat detected (true)
✅ "您好！看起来您可能没有说出您的问题或请求..." → chitchat detected (true)
✅ "当前日期和时间是2026年6月21日。如果您需要任何帮助..." → chitchat detected (true)
✅ "The user prefers dark mode..." → NOT chitchat (false) — valid summary preserved
✅ "User's name is Liuhao..." → NOT chitchat (false) — valid summary preserved
Passed: 9/9

$ grep -n 'CHITCHAT_PATTERNS\|isChitchatSummary' extensions/active-memory/index.ts
153:const CHITCHAT_PATTERNS = [
2535:function isChitchatSummary(value: string): boolean {
2553:    isChitchatSummary(singleLine)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** All 148 existing checks pass. All 6 observed chitchat variants from the issue are correctly detected and filtered. Legitimate memory summaries are not affected (2-pattern minimum prevents false positives).
- **What was not tested:** Live active-memory recall with real subagent — the fix is purely in the post-processing filter, verified through unit patterns and existing test suite.

target.title: fix(feishu): preserve self-mention <at> tag in parseFeishuMessageEvent to fix NO_REPLY in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Summary
Fix the Feishu multi-bot `NO_REPLY` bug: when multiple bots are @mentioned in the same group message, each bot could not see its own mention because `normalizeMentions` was called with `botOpenId` as `botStripId`, which removed the receiving bot's own `<at>` tag from `ctx.content`. The model would then conclude it was not addressed and return `NO_REPLY`.

The fix removes the `botOpenId` argument from the `normalizeMentions` call in `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, preserving all mentions as `<at>` tags. Command detection is unaffected:
- For groups, `normalizeFeishuCommandProbeBody` strips all `<at>` tags before command probing
- For p2p, `hasInlineCommandTokens` matches inline `/cmd` patterns regardless of leading `<at>` tags

Fixes #72504

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu bots in multi-bot group chats return NO_REPLY when @mentioned alongside other bots because `normalizeMentions` strips the receiving bot's own `<at>` tag from the content passed to the LLM.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13.1, Linux x64
- **Code analysis path**: See 5 Whys root cause analysis
- **Test framework**: Vitest

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.helpers.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts --run
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
...
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
...
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  87 passed (87)
```

Multi-bot scenario verified in new test "preserves all bot mentions in multi-bot group chat (#72504)":
```
@_bot_1 @_bot_2 check status
→ '<at user_id="ou_bot">Bot One</at> <at user_id="ou_bot_2">Bot Two</at> check status'
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All mentions are preserved as `<at>` tags in `ctx.content`. Each bot can see its own mention tag alongside other bots' mentions. The model receives the complete mention context and correctly identifies that it was addressed.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu tenant multi-bot group chat (requires physical Feishu app + multiple bot accounts with valid credentials). The fix was verified at unit-test level and by code-path analysis confirming command detection is unaffected.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 12/12 stripBotMention, 15/15 helpers, 87/87 bot |
| Type Check | ✅ | No new type errors |
| Lint | ✅ | oxfmt check passed |

## Risk checklist
**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes` - Bot's own `<at>` tag is now preserved in `ctx.content` (previously stripped). This changes the model-visible message body for the receiving bot.
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested

target.title: fix(doctor): stop scaffolding empty safeBinProfiles that override built-in defaults
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where `openclaw doctor --fix` silently weakened exec safe-bin security for anyone who added a built-in stdin-only helper (`cut`, `uniq`, `head`, `tail`, `tr`, `wc`, `grep`, `sort`, `jq`) to `tools.exec.safeBins`. Doctor scaffolded an empty `{}` profile for these bins, and because `resolveSafeBinProfiles` merges user profiles over the built-ins by key (not a deep merge), the empty `{}` wiped the built-in `maxPositional`, `allowedValueFlags`, and `deniedFlags`. The result was the worst of both worlds: the safe stdin-only form was rejected (e.g. `grep -e "pattern"`) while unsafe forms were allowed (e.g. `grep pattern file.txt`, `grep -r /`). The same empty-override hazard also lived in already-saved configs and was never repaired.

## Why This Change Was Made

doctor now distinguishes built-in bins from custom bins:

- Built-in bins are never scaffolded — their profile already applies at runtime. Plain `doctor` shows an informational note ("uses built-in profile (no custom override needed)") instead of a warning, and they are dropped from the `--fix` scaffold suggestion.
- Custom bins scaffold `{ maxPositional: 0 }` (a stdin-only default) instead of `{}`, matching the documented purpose of safeBins.
- The `safeBinProfiles` holder is created lazily, so doctor no longer leaves an empty `{}` object behind when nothing is scaffolded.
- An empty `{}` override of a built-in is flagged by plain `doctor` and **removed** by `doctor --fix` to restore the shipped profile. Detection is inheritance/fallback aware: it covers global scope, per-agent scope, agents that inherit the global `safeBins` list, and configs relying on the default safeBins set with no explicit `safeBins` key.

No config-shape, schema, or API change; no migration required (the runtime already reads the canonical shape — this only stops doctor from writing a harmful one and repairs existing harmful ones).

## User Impact

- Adding a built-in helper to `safeBins` keeps its built-in protections: the safe `-e`/stdin form works and unsafe file/recursive forms stay blocked.
- `doctor --fix` self-heals existing broken configs that contain an empty `{}` override of a built-in.
- Custom safeBins get a conservative stdin-only default scaffold instead of an unconstrained `{}`.

## Evidence

Tests (new + updated):

- `src/commands/doctor/shared/exec-safe-bins.test.ts`: built-in info note + no scaffold; custom `{ maxPositional: 0 }` scaffold; empty-override warn + `--fix` removal; explicit non-empty override preserved; global-`safeBins`-fallback (agent with no own safeBins) removal; default-safeBins (no `safeBins` key) detection; and a regression asserting `resolveSafeBinProfiles` returns the intact built-in `grep` profile after `--fix`.
- `src/commands/doctor/shared/preview-warnings.test.ts`: plain `doctor` warns about an empty built-in override even with no explicit `safeBins`.

Local validation (macOS):

- ~298 tests green across exec-safe-bins, preview-warnings, doctor-config-flow, repair-sequencing, security audit, exec-safe-bin policy/runtime, and agent-tools safe-bins.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` clean; `pnpm tsgo:core:test` clean for changed files (one unrelated pre-existing `packages/sdk/src/package.e2e.test.ts` error already on `main`).
- `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` clean on all changed files.

Review: the fix plan and the implementation were independently reviewed by Codex across three passes; all findings (inheritance/fallback detection gaps, message wording, and the plain-`doctor` preview gate for default-active safeBins) were addressed and confirmed resolved.

Notes: branched off latest `main`; independent of (and non-overlapping with) the other open PR on this fork.

target.title: fix #93908: [Bug]: [Bug]: Feishu plugin skipping discovery - host reports 2026.5.28 despite 2026.6.8 install
target.body:
Fixes #93908

## Summary

- Pin current-process post-core plugin update compatibility checks to the installed target OpenClaw version for both upgrades and downgrades.
- Pass the installed target version explicitly into mandatory post-core plugin convergence so it does not overwrite the target with the stale updater process `VERSION`.
- Add regression coverage for the 2026.5.28 -> 2026.6.8 Feishu/plugin API compatibility path.

## What Problem This Solves

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu/Lark plugin discovery can be skipped after a package update when post-core plugin update logic still evaluates plugin API compatibility against the pre-update host version. The reported Feishu package declares `openclaw.compat.pluginApi: >=2026.6.8`, but a stale updater process can still report host `2026.5.28`, causing discovery to skip the plugin with `plugin requires plugin API >=2026.6.8, but this host is 2026.5.28; skipping discovery`.
- Why it matters / User impact: Users who update OpenClaw to a Feishu-compatible target can still lose Feishu channel availability because the post-core plugin phase rejects the installed plugin using stale current-process host state. That makes message delivery through Feishu unavailable even though the installed OpenClaw package is new enough.
- What did NOT change: This does not change Feishu package metadata, plugin API ranges, semver compatibility enforcement, plugin manifest parsing, default install choices, or runtime fallback behavior for genuinely incompatible plugins.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The public contract surface remains `openclaw.compat.pluginApi` and the existing compatibility resolver. The source-of-truth for post-core update compatibility is the installed target package version because that is the gateway version that will run after restart; this patch only feeds that target version into the existing resolver boundary.
- Related open PR scan: Related open PR #94019 improves compatibility diagnostic guidance, but it does not change discovery/version resolution/update handoff behavior. This PR targets the same-contract root cause in the update handoff rather than duplicating diagnostic text changes.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The current-process post-core update path could still be running code whose module `VERSION` is the pre-update version (`2026.5.28`) while the installed package root had already been updated to `2026.6.8`. Fresh post-core child processes already receive `OPENCLAW_COMPATIBILITY_HOST_VERSION=<installed target>`, but the current-process path only pinned that override for downgrades. The convergence helper also overwrote inherited compatibility-host state with stale in-process `VERSION`.
- Why this is root-cause fix: Plugin compatibility during post-core update must be evaluated against the installed target package version, not the old updater process module state. Pinning `OPENCLAW_COMPATIBILITY_HOST_VERSION` to the installed target for the current-process plugin phase and passing the same target into convergence restores that lifecycle invariant, so the existing discovery gate makes the same decision the restarted gateway will make instead of masking the symptom with a Feishu-specific exception.

## Why This Change Was Made

The installed target package version is the canonical source of truth for post-core plugin compatibility. This PR scopes the override to the post-core plugin phase and restores the caller's environment afterward, so normal runtime version resolution and explicit operator overrides outside the update handoff are unchanged.

When the installed target package version cannot be read, convergence keeps its previous fallback to the current runtime `VERSION`.

## Verification

- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts --reporter=dot --runInBand` exited 0. `pnpm format:fix src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.ts src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts src/cli/update-cli.test.ts` exited 0; local Node printed the existing engine warning that the repo wants `>=22.19.0` while this machine has `v22.17.0`.
- Target test file: `src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts` covers the convergence helper using an explicit installed target over stale inherited host state; `src/cli/update-cli.test.ts` covers the current-process 2026.5.28 -> 2026.6.8 upgrade path pinning the environment during post-core plugin update and restoring it afterward.
- Scenario locked in: The regression tests lock in that a package update from host `2026.5.28` to installed target `2026.6.8` passes `2026.6.8` to plugin convergence and exposes `OPENCLAW_COMPATIBILITY_HOST_VERSION=2026.6.8` while `updateNpmInstalledPlugins` runs, even though the updater process itself is still old.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The focused tests exercise the update/convergence lifecycle boundary that owns the stale-version handoff, while the Feishu discovery proof exercises the real plugin API decision. No broad end-to-end network fixture is needed to prove that discovery no longer uses the stale host version.

## Real behavior proof

- Real environment tested: Windows local worktree, current OpenClaw source, real Feishu package metadata from this repo (`@openclaw/feishu` version `2026.6.8`, `openclaw.compat.pluginApi: >=2026.6.8`).
- Evidence after fix: With stale update-process state (`OPENCLAW_VERSION=2026.5.28`) and compatibility host pinned to `2026.5.28`, Feishu was not discovered and discovery emitted `plugin requires plugin API >=2026.6.8, but this host is 2026.5.28; skipping discovery`. With the same stale `OPENCLAW_VERSION=2026.5.28` but compatibility host pinned to the installed target `2026.6.8`, Feishu was discovered and no plugin API skip diagnostic was emitted.
- Observed result after fix: The decision boundary from #93908 changes from “stale host rejects Feishu” to “installed target host discovers Feishu”; this confirms the post-core compatibility host version is the variable that controls the reported skip.
- What was not tested: A Feishu network/message round trip was not completed. A temporary Feishu app credential was available, but this local environment could not complete the external `open.feishu.cn` fetch; the submitted proof covers the Windows/local discovery and update-version behavior that caused the skip.

## Regression Test Plan

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix for the update/plugin convergence version handoff; it changes the source-of-truth handoff used by existing compatibility checks rather than adding a Feishu-specific exception.
- Patch quality notes: The only type escape touched here is the pre-existing test style for constructing a minimal `OpenClawConfig` fixture (`as unknown as OpenClawConfig`); production code does not add a type escape. Patch-quality warning handling is explicit: this is not a guard/default/catch fallback, and the code changes the lifecycle source of truth used by existing compatibility checks.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for the stale-version handoff and discovery decision boundary because the production path has focused regression tests plus real Feishu metadata discovery proof. The only lower-confidence area is external Feishu network delivery, which is disclosed as not tested.

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk:availability`, `merge-risk:compatibility`, `merge-risk:update`, `merge-risk:plugin-runtime`.
- Risk explanation: Post-core update compatibility checks affect installed external plugin update/discovery behavior and therefore plugin availability after update. The change is intentionally narrow: during the post-core plugin phase, use the installed target package version that will run after restart. It does not relax compatibility checks, does not change public config/schema/protocol, and does not introduce a new config/env surface.
- Why acceptable: The fresh child process already uses this installed-target-version contract. This PR makes the current-process path and convergence helper follow the same contract, restores the previous environment after the scoped phase, and preserves the existing runtime `VERSION` fallback if the target version cannot be read.

## Non-goals

- Do not change Feishu package metadata or plugin API requirements.
- Do not relax or bypass plugin API compatibility checks.
- Do not add runtime fallbacks for incompatible plugins.

target.title: improve: ship 3D rendered lobster favicon for Control UI
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Control UI uses a flat, hand-drawn 2D lobster as its favicon and logo. This PR upgrades that mascot to a 3D-rendered version of the **same lobster**, giving the browser tab icon, the login screen logo, and the installed PWA icon a more polished, modern look without changing the brand identity.

## Why This Change Was Made

The four favicon assets in `ui/public/` are replaced in place, keeping the exact same filenames so no markup, manifest, or TypeScript changes are needed:

- `favicon.svg` — now a self-contained SVG that embeds the 3D bitmap as a base64 `data:` URI inside a single `<image>` element. Because the login page and agent logo already reference `favicon.svg`, they pick up the new art automatically.
- `favicon-32.png` — 32×32 raster of the 3D lobster.
- `apple-touch-icon.png` — 180×180 raster.
- `favicon.ico` — regenerated from the 3D art.

No external assets are introduced; `favicon.svg` remains a single, dependency-free file. `index.html`, `manifest.webmanifest`, and all referencing TS modules are untouched.

## User Impact

Users see the upgraded 3D lobster in their browser tab favicon, on the login screen, and as the installed PWA / Apple touch icon. No behavioral changes.

## Evidence

Before / after render of `favicon.svg` (2D vs 3D), both on a transparent background:

![favicon before and after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/woohahahaaa/openclaw/pr-assets/pr-assets/favicon-before-after.png)

Local validation:

- `pnpm build` — passed (favicon assets correctly copied into `dist/control-ui/`).
- `pnpm check` — passed except for the pre-existing `npm-shrinkwrap.json is stale` guard on `main`, which is unrelated to this change (no dependency or `package.json` edits were made; `npm-shrinkwrap.json` is untouched).
- Targeted favicon tests passed: `src/ui/public-assets.test.ts`, `src/ui/views/agents-utils.test.ts`.

## AI assistance

This change is **AI-assisted**. The 3D lobster artwork was generated with an AI image tool, and the asset pipeline (resizing to favicon sizes, embedding the bitmap into a self-contained SVG, ico generation) was carried out by an AI agent. The contributor confirms understanding of the change: it only swaps four static favicon assets in `ui/public/` with no code-path changes.

---

## Update — addressing Codex review (commit `9ce2bae`)

**1. `favicon.ico` regression — fixed.** The previous PR head encoded `favicon.ico` with only a single `(32,32)` entry, while `main` ships `[(16,16),(32,32),(48,48),(64,64),(128,128)]`. The ICO has been regenerated from the 256×256 source art and now matches `main` exactly with all five sizes.

**2. Real running-UI evidence — added.** The fork branch was cloned into an isolated temp dir, frontend deps installed, and the Vite dev server started. Before/after screenshots were captured from the **actually running Control UI dev server**, covering the three surfaces Codex called out — browser tab favicon, login-page logo, and PWA / Apple touch icon — plus a full 5-size `favicon.ico` breakdown:

![real running UI before/after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/woohahahaaa/openclaw/pr-assets/pr-assets/favicon-before-after-real.png)

The remaining open item is the **art-direction sign-off** on the 3D mascot, which requires maintainer design approval.

target.title: refactor(dispatch): gate reasoning delivery on a channel capability (follow-up to #95051)
target.body:
Small follow-up on top of this PR's branch (`qa-sre-telegram-reasoning-delivery-20260619`) — feel free to squash/cherry-pick into #95051 or ignore.

## Summary
#95051 gates reasoning-payload suppression on `deliveryChannel !== "telegram"`. This swaps that hardcoded channel name for a declared **`reasoningPayloads` channel capability**, mirroring the existing `resolveChannelTtsVoiceDelivery` precedent (`getChannelPlugin(id)?.capabilities.tts?.voice`). Channels with a reasoning lane opt in by declaring the capability instead of being named in core dispatch, so the next reasoning-capable channel (Slack, Matrix, …) needs no core edit.

- `ChannelCapabilities` gains `reasoningPayloads?: boolean` (next to `blockStreaming`).
- New `channelSupportsReasoningPayloads()` resolver — uses `getLoadedChannelPlugin` (loaded-only, no bundled cold-load) since it runs on every dispatch; fails closed for unloaded/unknown channels.
- Telegram declares `reasoningPayloads: true`; the suppression gate becomes `!channelSupportsReasoningPayloads(deliveryChannel)`.

**Behaviour-preserving:** Telegram still delivers durable reasoning, generic channels (WhatsApp/web) still suppress — the existing dispatch tests assert both, unchanged.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` → 0
- `vitest run dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "isReasoning"` + the new `reasoning-capabilities.test.ts` → green (telegram delivers via capability; generic suppressed)
- Reviewed with a Codex red-team pass (no blocking findings; verified the `getLoadedChannelPlugin` choice avoids a hot-path cold-load).

## AI assistance
Prepared with AI assistance (Claude). Behaviour verified locally on OpenClaw 2026.6.9-beta.1.

cc @Peetiegonzalez

target.title: fix #94919: [Bug]: Z.AI Coding-Plan: ECONNRESET triggers model fallback — fallback notice is invisible to the user in async contexts (cron jobs, sub-agents, isolated runs)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw generated `↪️ Model Fallback: ...` notices when a child/async run switched from the selected model to a fallback model, but async users could still miss the notice because ACP parent relays ignored fallback lifecycle events and cron/isolated output normalization dropped leading fallback notice payloads when preferring final assistant text.
- Solution: Surface the existing fallback lifecycle notice at the async parent/delivery boundaries instead of changing model selection or provider transport.
- What changed: ACP parent stream relay now forwards `fallback` and `fallback_cleared` lifecycle events to the parent session; cron/isolated payload normalization preserves leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads when it collapses text output to `finalAssistantVisibleText`.
- What did NOT change: No provider fallback decision logic, retry/backoff behavior, Z.AI endpoint handling, undici dispatcher defaults, public provider config, schema, or new `fetchOptions` contract.
- Why it matters / User impact: A user who starts a cron job, isolated run, or ACP/sub-agent run now receives the model-swap notice in the same async-visible stream/result instead of discovering the fallback only from a style/voice change.
- Fixes #94919

## What Problem This Solves

When fallback from the selected model to a configured runtime fallback already happened, the fallback notice was authored by the runner but could be lost at async delivery boundaries. The fix keeps the source-of-truth fallback decision in the runner and projects the existing notice metadata through the two boundaries that were dropping it: ACP child lifecycle events to parent stream, and cron/isolated final-output normalization.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Existing model fallback notices now cross the async boundary that was losing them. The proof below covers both sides of the patch: a real local OpenAI-compatible fallback execution (primary model failure followed by fallback model success) and the ACP parent-visible delivery path that turns the fallback lifecycle event into the notice the triggering user sees.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows Git Bash task worktree for this PR head (`959f27de19a0d93db8d76cd7d283277cb1cb105a`), pnpm-installed checkout, repo CLI executed with Node v22.19.0 via `npx -y node@22.19.0` because the repo requires Node `>=22.19.0`. The model fallback execution used isolated OpenClaw config/state and local OpenAI-compatible providers: `proof-fail/fail-model` intentionally failed on `127.0.0.1:9`, while `proof-ok/ok-model` returned `live proof ok`.
- **Exact steps or command shapes run after this patch:**
  ```sh
  cd <pr-worktree>
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<isolated-proof-config.json5> \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<isolated-proof-state-dir> \
  npx -y node@22.19.0 dist/entry.js agent --local --json \
    --session-key agent:main:live-proof-custom-ok-95370 \
    --message 'Reply with exactly: live proof ok' \
    --timeout 60
  ```
  The isolated config selected `proof-fail/fail-model` as primary and `proof-ok/ok-model` as fallback. The primary endpoint was deliberately unreachable; the fallback endpoint was a local OpenAI-compatible server returning a normal chat-completions response.

  ```sh
  cd <pr-worktree>
  ./node_modules/.bin/tsx <ACP fallback proof script>
  ```
  The ACP proof script imported the actual modules from this PR checkout (`startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay`, `emitAgentEvent`, and `drainSystemEventEntries`), started a parent relay for a child ACP session, emitted the same `stream: "lifecycle"` / `phase: "fallback"` event shape produced by model fallback execution, drained the parent session system-event queue, and asserted the user-visible fallback notice was present.

  ```sh
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec vitest run --reporter=verbose --config test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec vitest run --reporter=verbose --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts -t "relays model fallback lifecycle notices to the parent session"
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec oxlint src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  git diff --check
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** The local provider execution showed the primary model failed and the configured fallback model succeeded:
  ```text
  model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=proof-fail/fail-model candidate=proof-fail/fail-model reason=timeout next=proof-ok/ok-model detail=Connection error.
  model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=proof-fail/fail-model candidate=proof-ok/ok-model reason=unknown next=none
  [agent] run ... ended with stopReason=stop
  ```
  The corresponding JSON result included:
  ```json
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "live proof ok",
  "winnerProvider": "proof-ok",
  "winnerModel": "ok-model"
  ```
  Session records for the same triggering session first recorded a failed assistant turn from `proof-fail/fail-model` with `Connection error.`, then recorded the successful assistant response from `proof-ok/ok-model` with text `live proof ok`.

  The ACP parent-relay proof then produced these parent-visible system events:
  ```json
  {
    "parentSessionKey": "agent:main:live-proof-acp-parent-95370",
    "events": [
      {
        "text": "Started codex session agent:codex:acp:child-proof-95370. Streaming progress updates to parent session.",
        "contextKey": "acp-spawn:live-proof-acp-12428:start"
      },
      {
        "text": "codex: ↪️ Model Fallback: proof-ok/ok-model (selected proof-fail/fail-model; connection timeout)",
        "contextKey": "acp-spawn:live-proof-acp-12428:fallback"
      }
    ]
  }
  ACP_FALLBACK_LIVE_PROOF_OK
  ```
  This is the key user-visible assertion: after a fallback lifecycle event in the ACP child run, the triggering parent session receives the visible notice `codex: ↪️ Model Fallback: proof-ok/ok-model (selected proof-fail/fail-model; connection timeout)`.
- **Observed result after fix:** The fallback execution succeeded on the fallback provider after the selected provider failed, and the fallback lifecycle event was converted into a parent-visible ACP system event rather than staying child-local. The cron/isolated helper regression also confirms a leading `isFallbackNotice` payload is preserved before final assistant text when `finalAssistantVisibleText` replaces ordinary text payloads.
- **What was not tested:** This contributor environment did not complete an end-to-end hosted cron/gateway proof because the temporary local gateway scheduling path hit auth/pairing/scope issues. No live Z.AI/global endpoint credentialed outage was run, and no provider transport/fetch-options schema behavior was changed or tested.
- **Disclosure:** The fallback execution proof uses deterministic local OpenAI-compatible providers to exercise the real fallback chain. The ACP delivery proof executes the production ACP relay/event/system-event modules from this PR checkout and shows the parent-visible notice. Together they cover the PR’s propagation boundary; external provider transport policy and hosted cron scheduling remain out of scope for this patch.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 / RF-002 / RF-004 / RF-006 / RF-012:** ClawSweeper requested redacted real output proof from a cron, isolated, ACP, or sub-agent run showing the fallback notice reaches the triggering user. Addressed with the combined proof above: a real local OpenAI-compatible fallback execution shows primary failure and fallback success, and the ACP parent-relay proof shows the triggering parent session receives the `↪️ Model Fallback: ...` notice as a parent-visible system event.
- **RF-003 / RF-008:** The ACP relay is wired to the real fallback event shape emitted by `agent-runner.ts`: `stream: "lifecycle"`, `phase: "fallback"`, `selectedProvider`, `selectedModel`, `activeProvider`, `activeModel`, and `reasonSummary`. The proof uses `emitAgentEvent` and the parent system-event queue to show that shape is now visible to the parent session.
- **RF-004 / RF-007:** Maintainer policy confirmation is still acknowledged. This PR intentionally surfaces an already-generated internal status notice at async boundaries; if maintainers decide fallback notices should not be shown on some external surfaces, the narrow delivery points here are where that policy can be gated.
- **RF-005:** Guarded provider transport policy remains intentionally out of scope. This PR does not add or change provider connection options, dispatcher behavior, schemas, public config, or fallback selection logic.
- **RF-009 / RF-010 / RF-011:** Ran the requested `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` commands and `git diff --check`; all completed successfully in the PR checkout. Also reran the focused cron/ACP regressions with verbose Vitest output for maintainer-readable pass summaries.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts` covers the cron/isolated final-text collapse path, and `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts` covers ACP child-to-parent stream relay.
- **Scenario locked in:** A leading `isFallbackNotice` payload remains in `deliveryPayloads` when `finalAssistantVisibleText` replaces ordinary text payloads; a child `lifecycle:fallback` event with selected `zai/glm-5-turbo`, active `minimax/MiniMax-M3`, and reason `timeout` emits the formatted fallback notice to the parent session.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regressions exercise the exact async boundary functions that dropped visibility, without needing flaky external provider failures or live credentials. They fail if future code again treats fallback notices as child-local text during cron output selection or ACP parent stream relay.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** message-delivery, session-state, compatibility, security-boundary.
- **Risk explanation:** Message-delivery/session-state risk is real because this intentionally adds parent-visible status events and preserves fallback notice payloads in async delivery results. Compatibility risk is limited to surfacing a notice OpenClaw already generates. Security-boundary risk was reviewed because the issue also suggests provider transport config, but this PR does not add config/schema/dispatcher behavior or change SSRF/pinned dispatcher code.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is focused to async projection/delivery boundaries and adds no new provider API surface. It only moves an already-authored internal status notice to the user-visible async surface, with tests and real module proof showing the notice is preserved and formatted. Related open PR scan found public-config-related PRs, but this patch deliberately does not implement the public provider `fetchOptions` contract and does not overlap those config/schema changes.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Fallback notice generation and fallback state tracking were correct in the runner, but the async output boundaries did not preserve that status. `startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay()` ignored fallback lifecycle phases, and `resolveCronPayloadOutcome()` rebuilt text-only cron delivery from `finalAssistantVisibleText` without carrying leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The broken invariant was not provider failover itself; it was that an already-generated model fallback notice must reach the same async-visible output path as the final result. This patch fixes that invariant at the two source async projection owners: ACP lifecycle-to-parent relay and cron/isolated payload outcome selection. It does not mask the symptom downstream with string scraping or provider-specific Z.AI handling.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for notice propagation; medium for the broader issue because the separate per-provider connection-policy suggestion remains intentionally out of scope.
- **Patch quality notes:** The added `fallback`/`timeout` strings are test fixtures and the expected user-visible status text, not retry/default/fallback masking. The early `undefined` returns only skip malformed lifecycle events that cannot be formatted safely. The cron helper preserves only leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads, avoiding a broader status-notice delivery change.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source of truth for fallback decisions stays in `agent-runner.ts` / `fallback-state.ts`; ACP relay now projects the runner lifecycle event to the parent session, and cron payload outcome now preserves the existing payload metadata before final delivery. Public provider config/schema/protocol remains unchanged; the provider connection-policy/fetch-options contract suggested in the issue is explicitly out of scope for this PR.
